text.title: PLG Core Principles and Metrics
text.summary: Defines the four PLG principles, key 2026 metrics, onboarding best practices, and the evolution away from one-size-fits-all freemium.

rule.statement: Design for self-serve onboarding — users must activate without sales intervention.
rule.rationale: Sales-assisted onboarding caps growth by CAC payback; self-serve scales with zero marginal cost per new user.

rule.statement: Optimize for Time-to-Value (TTV): the "aha moment" must be reachable in under 5 minutes for consumer products.
rule.rationale: Users who do not experience value in the first session rarely return. TTV under 5 minutes is the industry threshold for consumer SaaS; developer products may allow more time for a first successful API call.

rule.statement: Build product virality — sharing and collaboration features must be intrinsic, not bolted on.
rule.rationale: Invitations and shared artifacts are the lowest-CAC acquisition channel when they trigger naturally from product usage.

rule.statement: Grow expansion revenue through usage, not sales calls — upsells must be triggered by hitting product limits.
rule.rationale: NRR above 120% allows a PLG company to grow revenue even while acquiring fewer new logos.

section.title: Key PLG Metrics

rule.statement: Track Activation Rate (% of signups completing the frozen activation event) and set a target above 40%.
rule.rationale: Activation rate is the primary leading indicator for D30 retention; if activated users do not retain materially better than non-activated, the activation definition is wrong.

rule.statement: Freeze the activation event definition in YAML, not in a dashboard; change only via PR with both PM and growth owner signing.
rule.rationale: Dashboard-stored definitions change silently quarter by quarter, making trend comparisons meaningless and causing PQL inflation.

section.p: Key metrics table: Activation Rate target >40%; TTV target <5 min; PQL tracking weekly; Expansion Revenue Rate target >30% of total; NRR target >120%.

rule.statement: Implement SSO/social login at step one to reduce signup drop-off before users see any value.
rule.rationale: Email-and-password forms at signup are the single highest-friction drop-off point in self-serve funnels.

rule.statement: Use interactive product tours, not documentation links; apply progressive feature disclosure rather than a full feature dump.
rule.rationale: Documentation links send users out of the product; a feature dump overwhelms new users and increases time-to-value.

rule.statement: Segment onboarding paths by user persona; personalize from first signup, not after the first session.
rule.rationale: One-size-fits-all onboarding fails all personas partially; a developer signing up has different activation triggers than a designer or a PM.

section.title: PLG Evolution 2026
section.p: One-size-fits-all freemium is declining. Tailored free tiers based on user personas, AI-driven personalization in onboarding, and hybrid PLG + sales-assist for enterprise accounts are the emerging patterns.

example.note: Ahrefs reached $40M ARR with 40 employees using pure PLG — the product itself was the sales motion.

antipattern.description: Limiting the free tier on signup count rather than on usage that correlates with willingness-to-pay.
antipattern.reason: Throttling new users hurts top-of-funnel; throttle on the dimension that scales with value (API calls, seats, storage) to align free-tier limits with upgrade intent.

section.reference: Frozen activation/PQL spec — the PM-to-growth contract
